title: CERT-In Direction (ii) 6-hour cybersecurity-incident reporting clock across 20 statutory categories with auto-submission to incident@cert-in.org.in
exclusions: Excludes near-misses and unsuccessful intrusion attempts that do not match any of the 20 statutory CERT-In categories. Excludes incidents reported on behalf of the organisation by a sectoral CERT (e.g., NCIIPC for Critical Information Infrastructure) where the sectoral CERT performs the CERT-In submission.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.62.1` archived hourly to `audit_evidence` with `cert_in_clause="CERT-In-Dir-2"`. Every incident pins (incident_id, cert_in_category, noticed_at, submitted_at, submission_age_minutes, submission_status, submission_evidence_ref, risk_score).

compliance.assurance_rationale: CERT-In Direction (ii) requires every service provider, intermediary, data centre, body-corporate, and government organisation to report cybersecurity incidents in any of the 20 statutory categories within 6 hours of noticing the incident. This UC operationalises both the trigger (incident categorisation against the 20-category taxonomy) and the deadline (the 6-hour clock from `noticed_at`) and proves the submission via the `submission_evidence_ref` (CERT-In Incident Reporting Form acknowledgement ID).
compliance.controlObjective: Demonstrates that every CERT-In-categoried incident is submitted to CERT-In within 6 hours of being noticed.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search archived to `audit_evidence`, the `CERT-In 6-hour Clock` dashboard, the CERT-In Incident Reporting Form acknowledgement export, the SOAR playbook execution record.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Section 70B(7) of the IT Act 2000 criminalises non-compliance with a CERT-In direction (imprisonment up to 1 year and/or fine). The 6-hour clock is the most commonly enforced limb. This UC's evidence trail demonstrates a defensible 'reasonable diligence' posture under Section 70B and is the primary defence against a Section 70B(7) prosecution. Full assurance would additionally require that the organisation has filed its POC (UC-22.62.3) and NTP synchronisation (UC-22.62.2) evidence.
compliance.controlObjective: Provides the incident-reporting limb of Section 70B compliance.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Open a ServiceNow SIR ticket at 09:00 IST with `cert_in_category = ransomware` and DO NOT submit to CERT-In until 16:00 IST. UC-22.62.1 MUST flag with risk_score=100 at the 6-hour boundary (15:00 IST) and again at every subsequent run.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Open the same ticket and trigger the SOAR playbook at 09:30 IST, with the CERT-In Incident Reporting Form submitted at 09:45 IST and the acknowledgement ID stored on the ticket. UC-22.62.1 MUST record risk_score=0.

dataSources: ServiceNow Security Incident Response (SIR) tickets ingested via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow with `cert_in_category` field. HEC `hec:certin:reporting:submission` for CERT-In Incident Reporting Form acknowledgements. KV Store `cert_in_category_taxonomy_lookup` (20 statutory categories with severity, examples, regulator-routing).
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk SOAR (Splunkbase 5664), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928).
spl:
index=servicenow_sir sourcetype="snow:incident" cert_in_category=*
| eval noticed_at = strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval six_hour_deadline = noticed_at + (6 * 3600)
| eval now_ts = now()
| join type=left number [
    search index=summary source=hec:certin:reporting:submission earliest=-7d
    | stats latest(_time) as submitted_at, latest(cert_in_ack_id) as submission_evidence_ref, latest(submission_status) as submission_status by incident_id
    | rename incident_id as number
  ]
| eval submission_age_seconds = case(
    isnotnull(submitted_at), submitted_at - noticed_at,
    1=1, now_ts - noticed_at)
| eval submission_age_minutes = round(submission_age_seconds / 60, 1)
| eval clock_status = case(
    isnotnull(submitted_at) AND submitted_at <= six_hour_deadline AND submission_status = "acknowledged", "on_time",
    isnotnull(submitted_at) AND submitted_at > six_hour_deadline, "late",
    isnull(submitted_at) AND now_ts > six_hour_deadline, "missed",
    isnull(submitted_at) AND now_ts <= six_hour_deadline, "in_window",
    1=1, "unknown")
| eval time_remaining_minutes = if(clock_status = "in_window", round((six_hour_deadline - now_ts) / 60, 1), null())
| eval risk_score = case(
    clock_status = "missed", 100,
    clock_status = "late", 90,
    clock_status = "in_window" AND time_remaining_minutes < 60, 70,
    clock_status = "in_window" AND time_remaining_minutes < 180, 40,
    clock_status = "in_window", 10,
    clock_status = "on_time", 0,
    1=1, 50)
| where risk_score > 0
| sort 0 -risk_score, +time_remaining_minutes
| table number cert_in_category noticed_at submitted_at submission_age_minutes time_remaining_minutes clock_status submission_evidence_ref risk_score
description: Pulls every ServiceNow SIR ticket tagged with `cert_in_category` (one of the 20 statutory CERT-In categories), computes the 6-hour deadline from `noticed_at`, and reconciles against the CERT-In Incident Reporting Form acknowledgements arriving at `hec:certin:reporting:submission`. Surfaces four clock states (`in_window`, `on_time`, `late`, `missed`) with risk-scoring tuned to escalate to CISO at 60 minutes remaining (risk=70) and to alert at 100 the moment the 6-hour deadline is breached without submission.
value: The 6-hour clock is the headline CERT-In obligation and the limb most commonly enforced. CERT-In has issued multiple notices to body-corporates citing late or missed reporting; criminal prosecution under Section 70B(7) of the IT Act 2000 carries imprisonment up to 1 year. The clock-running visualisation enables the SOC to escalate proactively before the 6-hour deadline rather than reactively after it, and the `submission_evidence_ref` (CERT-In acknowledgement ID) is the only auditable proof of submission.
implementation: (1) Maintain `cert_in_category_taxonomy_lookup` reflecting the 20 statutory CERT-In incident categories with regulator-routing (incident@cert-in.org.in plus sectoral CERT, if applicable). (2) Add a `cert_in_category` field to ServiceNow SIR via SOAR playbook auto-classification. (3) Hook the SOAR submission to the CERT-In Incident Reporting Form (https://www.cert-in.org.in/IRForm.htm) returning the acknowledgement ID via the HEC `hec:certin:reporting:submission` sourcetype. (4) Schedule UC-22.62.1 every 15 minutes during business hours and every 60 minutes off-hours; route `risk_score >= 90` to CISO at severity P1.
visualization: (Panel 1) Live 6-hour countdown for every open CERT-In-categoried incident. (Panel 2) Submission-time distribution. (Panel 3) Trend of missed / late submissions over 12 months. (Panel 4) Category-mix of reported incidents.

references.title: CERT-In FAQ on the 28 April 2022 Directions (May 2022 clarifications)

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Sectoral CERT routing**: incidents reported by NCIIPC on behalf of a CII organisation, or by a Sectoral CERT (e.g., CERT-Fin, CERT-Power), close the CERT-In obligation; load `sectoral_cert_ack_ref` in the ServiceNow ticket and treat as `on_time`. 2. **Duplicate report consolidation**: CERT-In permits consolidation of a series of related events into one report — the `consolidated_report_ref` should reference the originating ticket. 3. **Test / drill incidents**: tickets tagged `drill = true` are excluded from the alert pipeline.

**Suppression mechanism**: KV Store `cert_in_22_62_1_exceptions` with the CISO documented approval, an explicit incident_id + reason scope, and a written justification.
reviewer: Indian Cybersecurity SME Panel + CISO + Legal (CERT-In liaison)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

- ServiceNow Security Incident Response (SIR) integrated with Splunk via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow.
- Splunk SOAR available for automated submission of the CERT-In Incident Reporting Form.
- A registered CERT-In POC (UC-22.62.3 prerequisite) — only POC-authenticated submissions are accepted.

## Step 1 - CERT-In category taxonomy

```ini
[cert_in_category_taxonomy_lookup]
enforceTypes = true
field.cert_in_category = string
field.severity_default = string
field.regulator_routing = string
field.sectoral_cert = string
field.example_iocs = string
```

Seed the 20 statutory categories (illustrative — full list per the 28 April 2022 Directions): targeted scanning / probing of critical networks / systems; compromise of critical systems / information; unauthorised access of IT systems / data; defacement of website or intrusion into a website and unauthorised changes such as inserting malicious code, links to external websites, etc.; malicious code attacks such as spreading of virus / worm / Trojan / bots / spyware / ransomware / cryptominers; attack on servers such as database, mail, DNS, network appliances such as routers; identity theft, spoofing and phishing attacks; Denial-of-Service (DoS) and Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks; attacks on critical infrastructure, SCADA, OT systems; attacks on applications such as e-banking, e-commerce; data breach; data leak; attacks on IoT devices; attacks on systems related to digital payments; attacks through malicious mobile apps; fake mobile apps; unauthorised access to social media accounts; attacks or malicious / suspicious activities affecting cloud computing systems / networks / infrastructure / cloud-based applications; attacks or malicious / suspicious activities affecting big-data, blockchain, virtual asset / crypto-currency / cryptominers; attacks or malicious activities affecting AI / ML / IoT systems / applications.

## Step 2 - SOAR playbook integration

- Trigger: new ServiceNow SIR ticket with `cert_in_category` set.
- Action 1: Submit CERT-In Incident Reporting Form fields.
- Action 2: Receive acknowledgement-id back from CERT-In portal.
- Action 3: HEC submit to `hec:certin:reporting:submission` with `incident_id`, `cert_in_ack_id`, `submission_status`.
- Action 4: Update ServiceNow SIR with `cert_in_ack_id` and close the report-submission task.

## Step 3 - Schedule

```ini
[UC-22.62.1: CERT-In 6-hour Clock]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index, notable
action.summary_index._name = audit_evidence
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.62.1,reg=CERT-In,clause=CERT-In-Dir-2
alert.severity = 5
```

## Step 4 - Indian-jurisdiction-specific notes

- **Multi-jurisdiction organisations**: an Indian subsidiary of an offshore parent — only Indian-jurisdiction incidents fall under CERT-In; the parent SOC may need a co-routing flow.
- **NCIIPC overlap**: CII organisations report to both NCIIPC and CERT-In; the two reporting clocks are independent.
grandmaExplanation: Indian law gives every company 6 hours to tell the national cyber-incident team about serious cyber attacks. We watch the clock for every new incident and make sure the report is filed and acknowledged before the 6 hours run out.
